title: Catalysis
summary: Catalysis is the increase in rate of a chemical reaction due to a catalyst that is not consumed by the reaction. About 90% of all commercially produced chemicals involve catalysts at some stage.

facts.text: Catalysis is the increase in rate of a chemical reaction due to a catalyst; catalysts are not consumed by the reaction.
Approximately 90% of all commercially produced chemical products involve catalysts at some stage in their manufacture.
In 2005, catalytic processes generated about $900 billion in products worldwide.
The concept of catalysis was invented by chemist Elizabeth Fulhame and described in a 1794 book based on her work in oxidation–reduction reactions.
Catalysis is classified as homogeneous (catalyst and reactants in the same phase) or heterogeneous (catalyst and reactants in different phases).
Enzymes and other biocatalysts are often considered a third category of catalysis, distinct from homogeneous and heterogeneous.
The term 'catalyst' is derived from Greek καταλύειν meaning 'to annul', 'to untie', or 'to pick up'.

title: Distillation
summary: Distillation separates component substances of a liquid mixture by selective boiling and condensation. It accounts for approximately 25% of all industrial energy use.

facts.text: Distillation is the process of separating component substances of a liquid mixture by selective boiling of the mixture and condensation of the vapors.
Distillation accounts for approximately 25% of all industrial energy use due to its reliance on energy-intensive phase changes.
Distillation can operate over a wide range of pressures from 0.14 bar to nearly 21 bar.
Dry distillation (thermolysis/pyrolysis) is the heating of solid materials to produce gases that condense into fluid or solid products.
Idealized models of distillation are governed by Raoult's law (vapor pressure of solutions) and Dalton's law (sum of partial pressures).
In batch distillation, compounds are collected sequentially from most volatile to least volatile, with bottoms removed at the end.

title: Haber Process
summary: The Haber process (or Haber–Bosch process) converts atmospheric nitrogen to ammonia using hydrogen and an iron catalyst. It is the main industrial procedure for ammonia production.

facts.text: The Haber process converts atmospheric nitrogen (N₂) to ammonia (NH₃) by reaction with hydrogen (H₂) using finely divided iron metal as a catalyst.
Haber and assistant Robert Le Rossignol demonstrated the process in summer 1909, producing ammonia from air at about 125 mL per hour.
The process was purchased by German chemical company BASF; Carl Bosch scaled it to industrial production by 1910.
Fritz Haber received the Nobel Prize in 1918; Carl Bosch received the Nobel Prize in 1931 for their work on the process.
Ammonia production via the Haber process accounts for 1–2% of global energy consumption and 3% of global carbon emissions.
Atmospheric nitrogen comprises approximately 78% of the air but is exceptionally stable and does not readily react with other chemicals.
In 2012, 72% of global ammonia was produced from natural gas using the steam reforming process.
